attributes.stem:
A 47-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with fatigue and intermittent bloody diarrhoea. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She is diagnosed with inflammatory bowel disease and commenced on oral prednisolone and ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily.

At three-month follow-up, her bowel symptoms have significantly improved, but she reports ongoing fatigue and exercise intolerance. She experiences mild nausea with the iron tablets but continues to take them regularly.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Laboratory investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 85 g/L (115-155)
  • Ferritin 5 ng/mL (15-200)
  • Transferrin 500 mg/dL (200-400)
  • Transferrin saturation 2% (20-50)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has iron deficiency anaemia that has failed to respond to three months of oral iron therapy. Her inflammatory bowel disease appears well-controlled, but she has persistent severe anaemia with very low iron indices. Intravenous iron is indicated when there is failure to respond to oral iron therapy, particularly in inflammatory bowel disease where absorption may be impaired and ongoing losses occur.

Increasing oral ferrous sulfate dose is unlikely to be effective if standard doses have failed, and may worsen gastrointestinal side effects. The current dose is adequate, and poor response suggests an absorption or ongoing loss issue rather than inadequate dosing.

Adding vitamin C supplementation to enhance iron absorption is unlikely to overcome the fundamental issues of malabsorption and blood loss in inflammatory bowel disease. While vitamin C can improve iron absorption from dietary sources, it will not significantly impact therapeutic iron supplementation.

Increasing prednisolone dose would not address the iron deficiency. The patient's bowel symptoms have improved, suggesting adequate control of inflammation. The very low ferritin confirms true iron deficiency rather than anaemia of chronic disease.

Intramuscular iron administration is no longer recommended due to significant disadvantages including severe pain, permanent skin staining, and inconsistent absorption. Intravenous iron preparations are safer and more reliable.

Iron deficiency anaemia in inflammatory bowel disease often requires parenteral iron replacement due to factors such as inflammation affecting absorption, ongoing blood loss, and reduced oral iron tolerance. Modern intravenous iron preparations are safe and effective, allowing rapid repletion of iron stores.
learningPoints: Intravenous iron is indicated when oral iron therapy fails to improve iron indices after 3 months of adequate treatment.
Iron deficiency in inflammatory bowel disease often requires parenteral iron due to poor absorption and ongoing losses.
Intramuscular iron is no longer recommended due to pain, tissue staining, and risk of reactions.
references: Baird-Gunning J, Bromley J. Correcting iron deficiency. Aust Prescr. 2016 Dec;39(6):193-199. doi: 10.18773/austprescr.2016.069. Epub 2016 Dec 5. PMID: 27990046; PMCID: PMC5155066.
